title: Hair thinning and early baldness in men
excerpt: In Ayurveda, hair loss is read as the body's heat speaking through the scalp. Here is the classical view of khalitya and indralupta, and the honest limits of what oil and diet can do.
body_html:
what the texts call it

You notice it slowly. More hair on the comb, a wider parting, a forehead that seems to climb a little higher each year. For a lot of Indian men this starts in the twenties or thirties, and the mirror tells the story before anyone says a word.

Ayurveda has names for this. Khalitya is the gradual thinning and receding seen in many men, what we would now loosely call pattern baldness. Indralupta (also called indraluptaka) is the more sudden, patchy loss, the smooth coin-shaped bald spots that appear quickly. The classical texts list both among the disorders of the head and hair (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana; Madhava Nidana, on khalitya and indralupta).

So the experience you are having was described carefully more than a thousand years ago. That does not mean every cause back then is the cause now, but the framework is worth understanding.

too much internal heat, often pushed up by stress, anger, late nights, harsh sun, smoking, and very spicy, sour, salty or oily food, can scorch the hair roots over time

the pitta-and-hair view

Ayurveda treats hair (kesha) as a by-product, an upadhatu, tied to the bone tissue, asthi dhatu. Healthy bone metabolism shows up as healthy hair, which is why old vaidyas looked at the hair to read deeper nourishment.

The core idea for hair loss is heat. The texts say that pitta, the hot and sharp humour, travelling with vata into the channels of the hair roots, loosens the hair and prevents fresh growth in that spot (Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana; Bhavaprakasha, on khalitya). In plain terms: too much internal heat, often pushed up by stress, anger, late nights, harsh sun, smoking, and very spicy, sour, salty or oily food, can scorch the hair roots over time. Kapha and rakta (blood) getting involved is described as blocking the root so new hair cannot come through.

This is why the classical advice is rarely about the scalp alone. It is about cooling the system: calming pitta, steadying the mind, and keeping the blood clean. Bhavaprakasha and Madhava Nidana both place khalitya in the company of premature greying and scalp disorders that share this heat-driven story.

gentle everyday habits the texts support

Start with oiling, which Ayurveda calls a daily duty, not a luxury. Regular application of oil to the head is praised for strengthening hair roots and cooling the head (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, on daily routine). Cooling oils like coconut, and traditional preparations infused with amla and bhringaraj, were favoured for hair specifically.

Then cool the inside. Reduce the very hot, sharp, sour and fried foods that aggravate pitta, and lean towards cooling, sweet and bitter foods, plenty of water, and amla, which Bhavaprakasha Nighantu praises as a rasayana good for hair and greying. Eat on time and do not skip meals; erratic eating disturbs the digestion that ultimately feeds the hair.

Protect the head from harsh sun, avoid very hot water on the scalp, and treat sleep and stress as part of the treatment, not separate from it. A calmer mind is a cooler head, and the texts are clear that mental heat reaches the hair roots too. None of this is dramatic. It is the slow, steady cooling that Ayurveda always favours over force.

the honest limits, and when to see a doctor

Be honest with yourself about what habits can and cannot do. Where thinning follows a strong family pattern and a clear receding line, oiling and diet may slow things and keep what you have healthier, but they are unlikely to regrow a fully bald, shining scalp. The texts themselves treated long-standing khalitya as difficult. Anyone promising guaranteed full regrowth from a bottle is selling you hope, not Ayurveda.

See a doctor or a qualified vaidya if hair loss is sudden, patchy, or spreading fast, if the bald skin is red, scaly, itchy or scarred, if hair is also falling from eyebrows or beard, or if it comes with tiredness, weight change or other symptoms, since these can point to thyroid issues, anaemia, fungal infection, alopecia areata or nutritional deficiency that need proper testing. Hair loss after a major illness, fever, crash diet or new medicine is also worth a check.

This article is general information drawn from the classical texts, not a diagnosis or a prescription, and it is not a substitute for examination and care by a qualified professional.

Try This Today

  • oil the head, calmly: A few times a week, gently massage cooling oil (coconut, or an amla or bhringaraj oil) into the scalp and leave it on for a while before washing. Ashtanga Hridaya treats head oiling as a daily routine that strengthens roots and cools the head.
  • cool the plate: Cut back on very spicy, sour, salty, fried and oily food that aggravates pitta. Lean towards cooling, sweet and bitter foods, drink enough water, and add amla in some form, praised as a hair-friendly rasayana.
  • treat the mind as part of the scalp: Protect sleep, ease daily stress and anger, and keep a steady meal schedule. A calmer, cooler system reaches the hair roots; the texts link mental heat directly to hair loss.
  • skip the harsh stuff: Avoid very hot water on the scalp, long harsh-sun exposure, and aggressive chemical treatments. Be skeptical of any product promising guaranteed full regrowth.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: In Ayurveda, hair loss is read as the body's heat speaking through the scalp. Here is the classical view of khalitya and indralupta, and the honest limits of what oil and diet can do.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Ayurveda calls gradual male thinning khalitya and sudden patchy loss indralupta, both linked to excess heat (pitta) at the hair roots.
Cooling habits help: regular head oiling, less spicy and fried food, amla, good sleep and lower stress.
Oil and diet can protect and slow loss, but strong pattern baldness rarely fully regrows; sudden, patchy or scaly loss needs a doctor.

glossary_terms.definition: Gradual thinning and receding of scalp hair in men, close to pattern baldness.
Sudden, patchy hair loss leaving smooth coin-shaped bald spots.
The hot, sharp humour; its excess is the main culprit in Ayurvedic hair loss.
Hair; treated as an upadhatu (by-product) connected to bone tissue.
The bone tissue, whose nourishment is mirrored in healthy hair.
A rejuvenating, nourishing substance or therapy; amla is a classic example for hair.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Nidanasthana, on khalitya and indralupta
Madhava Nidana, section on khalitya and indralupta (kshudraroga)
Bhavaprakasha, on khalitya; Nighantu, on amla as a hair rasayana
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, on dinacharya (head oiling)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Khalitya" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Khalitya is defined as: Gradual thinning and receding of scalp hair in men, close to pattern baldness.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to oil the head, calmly. A few times a week, gently massage cooling oil (coconut, or an amla or bhringaraj oil) into the scalp and leave it on for a while before washing. Ashtanga Hridaya treats head oiling as a daily routine that strengthens roots and cools the head.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to cool the plate. Cut back on very spicy, sour, salty, fried and oily food that aggravates pitta. Lean towards cooling, sweet and bitter foods, drink enough water, and add amla in some form, praised as a hair-friendly rasayana.
